facts.question: Can you ping the default gateway? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: This checks whether your device can reach your local router. If not, internet usually cannot work.

facts.question: Can you ping the default gateway? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: If the gateway responds, your local network is likely alive and the problem may be upstream.

facts.question: Is DNS server reachable? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: DNS translates website names to IP addresses. If DNS is down, browsing appears broken.

facts.question: What is the interface status? (up/down)
facts.user_explanation: This tells whether your network adapter is active. 'down' means local link is not ready.

facts.question: Can you ping the target LAN device? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: If only one device is unreachable while the gateway works, that device may be offline.

facts.question: Average latency in milliseconds?
facts.user_explanation: Higher latency means packets take longer to travel, which often indicates congestion.

facts.question: Channel interference level in dBm (less negative is worse, e.g. -60)?
facts.user_explanation: Wi-Fi interference from nearby networks/devices causes unstable and slow wireless performance.

facts.question: Is the modem/ONT online? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: If the modem is offline, your connection to the ISP is usually unavailable.

facts.question: Does the WAN interface have a public IP assigned? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: No public IP often means your modem/router is not successfully connected to ISP provisioning.

facts.question: Does your ISP status page report an outage in your area? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: If the ISP confirms an outage, local troubleshooting usually won't restore service immediately.

facts.question: Observed packet loss percentage to an internet host?
facts.user_explanation: Packet loss means dropped data packets; high values often cause slow or broken internet apps.

facts.question: Does traceroute stop inside your ISP network? (yes/no)
facts.user_explanation: If traceroute dies in the ISP path, the issue is likely outside your home LAN.
